narrative:
Monday is a head-spinning, memorable weather day. We start with mild temperatures, rain, and Blue Ridge fog. A strong cold front moves through late this morning with a line of thunderstorms. This afternoon, temperatures fall off a cliff—sharply colder. Precipitation changes to snow (brief sleet possible) from west to east and top down during the afternoon. Snow showers continue into tonight, especially on the west side of the Appalachians. Snow showers (west) and flurries continue into Tuesday morning. Snow accumulations range from a dusting at lower elevations near the Blue Ridge (east) to moderate accumulations at higher elevations along the west edge of the mountain region. See our Winter Weather Event page or details on forecast accumulations and then snow reports as the event unfolds.

Tuesday is impressively cold for St. Patrick’s Day, but the wind settles. Wednesday is less cold by the afternoon. A gradual warm-up begins Thursday.

The "Mix and Match" Special Offer continues on all the Snowman O'Mometer Pint Glass and Mug (or you can long for a 5 Golfball Day with that pint glass as well). Buy one, and get another drinkware item at 40% off.

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Winter Weather Alert:
Monday-Tuesday

Rain, Thunder, Snow

Last Updated Mon March 16 at 10:56AM by Ray Russell

This afternoon, temperatures fall off a cliff—sharply colder. Precipitation changes to snow (brief sleet possible) from west to east and top down during the afternoon. Forecast Discussion

A strong front moves through around noon. We start the day with Blue Ridge fog and rain. A thunderstorm arrives later this morning ahead of the frontal passage. Temperatures remain relatively mild through the morning with a gusty southwest wind.

This afternoon, temperatures fall faster than you might think possible from west to east as the wind shifts to the northwest. Precipitation changes from rain to snow with a brief period of sleet in the transition. The transition begins first along the western escarpment and reaches the eastern edge of the mountain region by mid to late afternoon. Snow showers and windy conditions continue into tonight. I leave the details on snow accumulations to ou Winter Weather Event Page.

Snow showers (west) and flurries continue into Tuesday. Tuesday is impressively cold for St. Patrick’s Day. Skies clear by sunset.

Wednesday, high pressure is centered over Virginia. Our flow becomes more southwesterly. The day is cold for March, but not nearly as cold by Wednesday afternoon.

High pressure controls the Southeast for the rest of this week. Temperatures become progressively warmer each day. The weekend will be almost as pleasant as Tuesday is cold. What a contrast! Warm fronts pass to our east in this milder flow. We might see a stray shower at some point beginning Friday night, but for now, I keep the forecast dry.

A weak front moves through from the north Sunday night, putting a slight dent in our warm temperatures early next week.

Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters.

Latest Insights
Mid-March Madness

A strong cold front crosses the Southern Appalachians Monday morning and moves into the Foothills Monday afternoon.

Rain and thunderstorms accompany the frontal passage, and severe thunderstorms are ...

Mar 15, 2026
Severe Weather Season Around The Corner

As our March weather has become more changeable and volatile, it's a reminder that severe weather season is almost upon us.

Last week was Severe Weather Awareness Week in North Carolina; This week is Virginia's turn.

While western North Carolina and Southwest Virginia may have less overall severe weather than our surrounding neighbors, we still have our fair share, and it's a great time to review your severe weather emergency/preparedeness procedures ahead of time.

Mar 13, 2026
It is, after all, March....

March Madness rules supreme! After record high temperatures on Wednesday, temperatures at noon today are around 40 degrees colder vs. this time on Wednesday.

Oh, and yeah, it snowed in the mountains this morning!

Expect more wild swings this weekend and early next week.
